choices.text: Frame it as the victim bringing trouble
Frame it as pressure and circumstance
Frame it as someone trying to protect something
Frame it as something that got out of hand

nodes.text: I notice patterns for a living. Movement near {place} stood out because it did not fit the hour or the posture of everybody else on shift.
{time_phrase}. Close enough to the schedule break that I remember exactly what part of the shift I was in.
I knew the victim the way staff know regular trouble: by rhythm, not intimacy.
You are treating operational memory like confession. They are not the same discipline.
Exact location? Near the service angle, where people who know a building stop behaving like visitors.
I have seen people make the same bad choice in six different uniforms. That does not make the outcome inevitable, only familiar.
Circumstance is just pressure with paperwork. Short staffing, debt, favors, access, humiliation. It all looks operational until it turns violent.
People do reckless things for coworkers, family, and the stories they tell themselves about loyalty.
Accidental is what incident reports say when no one wants to write down the chain of decisions that made the scene possible.

choices.text: Ask whether someone tried to fix it after

nodes.text: The impact may have been sudden. The setup rarely is.
That is the part I expect. People start managing appearances the second they realize the incident will outlive them.
